author: @pw601
text:
Thank you for posting this.  I used the technique to zinc plate an area of steel that had been used to repair a rusty perforation in a  silencer that was chromed.  A new part was unobtainable.  I buffed up the zinced area to a nice shine so from a distance it's not noticeable.
I have used a bucket technique with a plating kit before, but, as you say this part was too big to do this and the repaired area small. I'm really pleased with the result. I'm now looking around for other things to do!

author: @PrisonLaParis
text: Great video!

author: @RWBHere
text: You can wand plate a clean Iron surface with Copper without a power supply. Degrease the Iron, take a swab soaked in Copper Sulphate solution and rub it onto the Iron. The Copper is deposited very quickly in a thin layer which is ready for Nickel plating. I hope that this is useful to someone reading this. 🙂

author: @jorgemartin7697
text:
Dear Robert, could I use the violet cobalt chloride from cat litter to galvanize metal parts?
Or could I use it as a colorant for vitreous enamels?
Thank you

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am afraid I don't know mate

replies.author: @jorgemartin7697
replies.text:
Desmond Bagley you may be thinking of bentonite sand, but I mean silicon dioxide sand, which looks like coarse salt crystals and also has other violet crystals, which is precisely the one that contains cobalt chloride, and it serves As an indicator of humidity, I will carefully read your suggestions
Thank you
